article_title: Tesla Debuts Third-Generation Optimus at Shanghai Expo, Targets Mass Production by Year-End
summary: Multiple claims attributed to cited sources cannot be verified from those sources. Several specific details (pricing, production targets, facility plans, quotes) appear unsupported or misattributed.

findings.message: Multiple claims attributed to Fortune source cannot be verified
findings.details: The Fortune article does not contain: (1) the quote describing Gen 3 as 'by far the most advanced robot in the world,' (2) the 'final stages' characterization, (3) the $20,000-$30,000 target price, (4) the one million units/year capacity target at Fremont, (5) Model S/X assembly line conversion details, (6) the end-of-2027 public sales timeline. The Fortune article only confirms the 'agonizingly slow' production pace quote.

findings.message: Claims attributed to TechCrunch source could not be fully verified
findings.details: The TechCrunch article's body content could not be fully extracted for verification. The 'agonizingly slow' quote appears to originate from the Fortune article, not TechCrunch, raising a misattribution concern. The claim that 'Tesla had produced only hundreds of Optimus units despite earlier pledges to build thousands' could not be confirmed from the available content.

findings.message: Rest of World source partially supports claims but several details are not present
findings.details: The Rest of World article confirms China's 90%+ share of humanoid robot sales and Lian Jye Su's analyst assessment. However, it does not specifically mention AWE 2026 Shanghai, does not name Unitree/Agibot/Leju in the extracted content, and does not discuss 'Giga Shanghai' as a proving ground for Optimus.

Detailed specs (22 degrees of freedom, 25 actuators per forearm/hand, tendon-based actuator system) are not attributed to any of the three cited sources. These appear to come from Tesla press materials or other sources not cited in the article.

content_preview.title: Tesla Debuts Third-Generation Optimus at Shanghai Expo, Targets Mass Production by Year-End
content_preview.summary: Tesla showcased its first mass-production-ready humanoid robot at AWE 2026 in Shanghai, with plans to begin manufacturing this summer and sell units to the public by the end of 2027.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Tesla unveiled its third-generation Optimus humanoid robot at the Appliance & Electronics World Expo (AWE 2026) in Shanghai on March 12, marking the company's first public showing of what it describes as a mass-production-ready humanoid machine. The exhibit, which also featured the Cybertruck, signals Tesla's deepening commitment to the Chinese market and its ambition to pivot from automaker to robotics manufacturer.

## What We Know

The Gen 3 Optimus has been redesigned from first...

recommendations: Add sources that actually support the specific claims about pricing ($20,000-$30,000), production capacity (one million units at Fremont), facility conversion (Model S/X lines), and the 2027 public sales timeline
Correct or remove the 'most advanced robot in the world' and 'final stages' quotes if they do not originate from the cited Fortune article
Add a source for the technical specifications (22 degrees of freedom, actuator counts)
Add a source that covers the AWE 2026 Shanghai showcase directly
Verify and correct the attribution of the 'agonizingly slow' quote — it appears in Fortune, not TechCrunch

editor_notes.source_verification: 1. Rest of World — PARTIALLY VERIFIED. Confirms China >90% humanoid robot sales and Lian Jye Su (Omdia) analyst assessment. Does NOT mention AWE 2026, does NOT name Unitree/Agibot/Leju, does NOT discuss Giga Shanghai as Optimus proving ground. 2. Fortune — POORLY VERIFIED. Only confirms 'agonizingly slow' quote. Does NOT contain: 'most advanced robot in the world' quote, 'final stages' quote, $20K-$30K pricing, 1M unit/year Fremont capacity, Model S/X line conversion, 2027 public sales timeline. 3. TechCrunch — UNABLE TO FULLY VERIFY. Article body not fully extractable. 'Agonizingly slow' quote appears in Fortune not TechCrunch (misattribution). 'Hundreds of Optimus units' claim unconfirmed.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Numerous specific factual claims (pricing, production targets, facility conversion plans, direct quotes) are attributed to the three cited sources but cannot be verified from them. The article appears to contain information from uncited sources or potentially fabricated details.
editor_notes.content_quality: The article structure (Overview / What We Know / What We Don't Know) is well-organized and the writing is clear and neutral. Word count (499) is within News category range (400-1200).
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Tone is appropriately neutral. Musk claims are properly attributed and balanced with skeptical context about Tesla's track record on timelines.
editor_notes.originality: No duplicate articles found. Related Tesla and humanoid robot articles exist but cover different topics (Terafab, NHTSA, Xiaomi robots, Apptronik).
concerns: Fortune source does not contain most of the claims attributed to it (pricing, capacity targets, facility plans, quotes)
TechCrunch quote attribution appears incorrect — 'agonizingly slow' quote is from Fortune
Technical specifications (22 degrees of freedom, actuator counts) have no cited source
AWE 2026 Shanghai event is not covered by any of the three cited sources
Rest of World source does not mention specific Chinese competitors named in the article
editor_notes.overall_assessment: REQUEST_CHANGES. The article violates editorial policy by making claims that cannot be traced to cited sources. The submission needs additional sources that actually support the specific claims, or unsupported claims must be removed.
